why_winner: The split-screen opening ('Two developers. Same AI tools. Completely different outcomes.') is the strongest hook — it immediately frames the stakes and invites the reader to identify which developer they are. The parallel structure makes the core contrast visceral and scannable, directly matching the performance signal that comparison framing drives the highest engagement with this audience.

runner_up_notes.A: The story hook creates real narrative tension ('My answer took them by surprise'). The revised C should borrow this tension by hinting at an unexpected outcome for Developer B — not just 'confusion' but something the reader wouldn't expect from using the same tools as everyone else.

issues.description: The arrow characters '→' in the split-screen list items may render inconsistently across LinkedIn clients and mobile. LinkedIn is plain-text; Unicode arrows can look out of place compared to simple dashes or line breaks.
issues.action: Replace '→' with plain dashes or remove the bullet marker entirely — let the line items stand on their own as clean parallel lines. The structure reads fine without the arrow.

issues.description: 'Developer A' and 'Developer B' labels are slightly clinical and distancing. They read more like a textbook example than a real observation.
issues.action: Reframe the split-screen headers in a way that feels like something Altaf actually observed — e.g., 'The developer with strong foundations:' / 'The developer without them:' or drop the labels and let the parallel lists speak for themselves.
